title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at an after-school homework club. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Beth, The Maths Helper
Beth volunteered at the homework club because she wanted to make maths feel less frightening and mysterious than it had once felt to her at school. [47]She began every session by asking pupils to show where their working went wrong, instead of asking only for the final answer.[/47] Her accountancy job helped her explain percentages through household bills, discounts and football statistics. She expected quick progress, but some pupils needed several patient weeks before admitting they were lost. [48]The moment she still remembers best was when a quiet boy corrected her deliberately planted calculation mistake and then laughed at his own confidence.[/48] Beth learned to praise careful checking more than speed. She also discovered that coloured pens reduced panic for several children who hated crossing out. Now she thinks the club’s real achievement is giving pupils a place where confusion is treated as useful information, not failure or laziness.
texts.B:
### Aaron, The Snack Coordinator
Aaron joined the homework club after the youth centre manager asked for help with food, not teaching, on Tuesday afternoons. [49]He soon realised that the simple snack table determined whether pupils arrived tired and irritable or ready to concentrate for an hour.[/49] His previous catering experience helped him plan cheap, filling options that avoided too much sugar before study. [50]The main challenge was working around allergies without making any child feel singled out or embarrassed.[/50] Aaron labelled ingredients discreetly and clearly and kept spare fresh fruit in a separate box. He was surprised by how many pupils began talking to him while waiting for toast. Those small conversations helped staff quickly notice who had fallen out with close friends or forgotten lunch. Aaron says the club taught him that care can be practical and quiet. Nobody came for his sandwiches, he jokes, but sometimes they willingly stayed because of them.
texts.C:
### Miriam, The Reading Mentor
Miriam patiently helped pupils who avoided reading aloud whenever possible. She had been invited by a librarian who knew she once recorded audiobooks for people with visual impairments locally. [51]That experience helped her teach students to follow punctuation as a guide for breathing, rather than racing through every sentence.[/51] Miriam never forced anyone to read in front of the whole noisy room. [52]Her hardest task was convincing one student that choosing an easier book was not a defeat, but a safe way back into reading.[/52] Together, they started with graphic novels and gradually moved to short detective mysteries. Miriam loved seeing pupils confidently recommend books to each other with the seriousness of experienced experts. She kept a small red notebook of words that caused trouble, then built games around them the following week after school. She says reading confidence often grows sideways, through jokes, pictures and shared secrets about characters.
texts.D:
### Khalid, The Exam Planner
Khalid volunteered during the busiest exam season, helping older pupils plan realistic revision without staying up all night revising. [53]He created a wall calendar where deadlines, sports fixtures and family events and birthdays were written together, so students could clearly see their real available time.[/53] His engineering background at a factory made him comfortable breaking large tasks into small, visible stages. [54]He was surprised that the most anxious pupils often had the neatest notes but no clear plan for using them.[/54] Khalid introduced short timed practice blocks and insisted on breaks. One memorable evening, a student brought in a timetable she had designed herself, complete with proper rest days. [55]He now gives parents a short plain explanation sheet so they understand why constant extra revision sessions may not actually help.[/55] [56]He wants the club to start exam planning earlier in the year, before panic has already taken over the house.[/56]

questions.question: asks students to examine their process rather than only provide an answer?
questions.explanation: Beth asks pupils to show where their working went wrong instead of asking for the final answer.

questions.question: remembers a student gaining confidence by noticing a deliberate error?
questions.explanation: Beth remembers a quiet boy correcting her planted mistake and laughing confidently.

questions.question: realised food affected whether students could settle into study?
questions.explanation: Aaron found the snack table influenced whether pupils arrived ready to concentrate.

questions.question: had to handle dietary restrictions sensitively?
questions.explanation: Aaron worked around allergies without making any child feel singled out.

questions.question: used recording experience to teach students how punctuation can support reading aloud?
questions.explanation: Miriam used audiobook experience to teach students to use punctuation as a breathing guide.

questions.question: helped someone accept a simpler text as a positive step?
questions.explanation: Miriam convinced a student that choosing an easier book was a way back into reading.

questions.question: created a planning display that included school tasks and personal commitments together?
questions.explanation: Khalid made a wall calendar showing deadlines, sports fixtures and family events.

questions.question: noticed that some worried students had tidy materials but no strategy?
questions.explanation: Khalid found anxious pupils often had neat notes but no plan for using them.

questions.question: explains the value of limits to parents as part of the project?
questions.explanation: Khalid gives parents a sheet explaining why constant extra revision may not help.

questions.question: wants one form of support to begin before stress becomes severe?
questions.explanation: Khalid wants exam planning to start earlier in the year before panic takes over.
